works.name: Harold Hotelling, "The Economics of Exhaustible Resources" (1931)
works.how_engaged: Hotelling prices the private owner's scarcity rent (when should I sell my oil; rent rises at rate of interest). The framework adds the Hotelling Identity, RCV − Hotelling rent = CS per unit: commons-extraction cost is precisely the residual value beyond standard Hotelling rent. Hotelling optimizes the extraction path for the resource owner, not for humanity; he structurally excludes intergenerational foreclosure cost because future-generation utility is not in the owner's objective function. RCV prices what Hotelling's model cannot capture. Explicitly framed as the framework's part vs Hotelling's part.

works.name: A.C. Pigou, The Economics of Welfare (1920)
works.how_engaged: Pigou prices costs imposed on third parties via pricing instruments (the externality framework). The framework treats Pigouvian instruments (carbon taxes, cap-and-trade, reclamation bonds) as covering externalities only PARTIALLY; the Theorem P2 ("partial externality pricing — extension of Pigou 1920") establishes that externality components of the bond B are strictly less than corresponding RCV components, forcing aggregate B < aggregate RCV. Hotelling Identity is positioned against Pigou-only (which treats resource-rent as private revenue and prices only the externality tail). CAI sub-form routes private-property-externality claims OUT to the Pigou tradition.

works.name: Ronald Coase, "The Problem of Social Cost" (1960)
works.how_engaged: Theorem premise P1 ("intergenerational market incompleteness — extension of Coase 1960"): Coasean complete bargaining requires all affected parties to participate, but future generations cannot bid in present markets, so market pricing systematically under-internalizes intergenerational cost components. Applied symmetrically to backward damages (CSD − B₁). Coase also named as one of the standard single-accounting-object environmental-economics frameworks the two-instrument architecture is rejected against.

works.name: Elinor Ostrom, Governing the Commons (1990); Hess & Ostrom, Understanding Knowledge as a Commons (2007)
works.how_engaged: Ostrom's eight design principles govern RENEWABLE coordination-commons (fisheries, forests, irrigation) where there is a harvest rate to manage; they give nothing for the permanent foreclosure of a non-renewable barrel of oil. The framework does NOT subsume Ostrom and Ostrom does not subsume the framework — hybrid cases admit parallel analyses. RCV is read as Ostrom's principles translated into a pricing mechanism for the non-renewable case ("what does it cost when management fails and it doesn't last?"). The CCI sub-form routes coordination-commons claims OUT to Ostrom. Ostrom also cited as precedent for the Option C' illustrative-not-canonical list discipline. Also flagged as not reaching the heterogeneous/structurally-divided-stakeholder commons.

works.name: John Hartwick, "Intergenerational Equity..." (1977) (Hartwick Rule)
works.how_engaged: Hartwick: invest all resource rents in reproducible capital for a non-declining consumption path (Norway's GPFG is the cleanest application). Hartwick assumes strong substitutability (S_max = 1). RCV generalizes Hartwick to S_max ≤ 1: when substitution is imperfect, no investment policy can fully compensate for foreclosure — strategic preservation is the only complete solution. Hartwick's Rule is the investment policy that makes CS = 0 only under S_max = 1.

works.name: Martin Weitzman, "Gamma Discounting" (2001)
works.how_engaged: The framework ADOPTS Weitzman declining discount rates directly as its D(t,t₀) factor — under uncertainty about the true rate, the certainty-equivalent rate declines toward the lowest plausible rate. Framed as the most significant methodological choice; biases RCV downward, making results conservative. Used to adjudicate the Nordhaus (5.5%) vs Stern (1.4%) debate: CS > 0 holds across the entire plausible rate range, only magnitude varies.

works.name: John Rawls, A Theory of Justice (1971) + Just Savings Principle
works.how_engaged: RCV is "the Rawlsian veil applied to resource extraction": if you did not know which generation you'd be born into, would you consent to first-come extraction of the planet's non-renewable endowment? Implements Rawls's "no grounds for discounting future well-being on pure time preference" via the U(R,t,Q(t)) term giving future generations equal standing in the integral. Names Rawls's limit: Just Savings is synchronic-leaning and underdeveloped (Rawls's own admission); Brian Barry's narrowing-of-opportunities sharpening is what RCV prices directly as foreclosure cost. Rawls's primary goods also cited as precedent for the illustrative-list discipline.

works.name: Herman Daly — ecological economics / strong sustainability (1990s)
works.how_engaged: Daly argues normatively that weak sustainability (capital-for-natural-capital substitution) is insufficient; strong sustainability requires maintaining critical natural-capital stock. RCV operationalizes Daly mathematically: where Daly argues S_max must equal 1, RCV asks empirically what S_max is per resource class, quantifying the cost when S_max < 1 (rare earths, PGMs, phosphorus). Complements Daly's qualitative argument with a measurable framework.

works.name: David Harvey, The New Imperialism (2003) — accumulation by dispossession
works.how_engaged: Harvey names the broader mechanism (capital accumulates by dispossessing communities of commons through enclosure, privatization, force). Cost severance is the PRICING dimension of that mechanism — the financial means by which dispossession appears economically rational. "Harvey diagnoses the mechanism; RCV measures its magnitude." Accumulation-by-dispossession says it happened; the IPG says by how much ($518 of cost absorbed per $4.50 paid, McDowell County). Measurement does not replace political analysis; it gives it a number.

works.name: William Darity & A. Kirsten Mullen, From Here to Equality (2020); stratification economics (Darity, Hamilton et al.)
works.how_engaged: Reparations-economics methodology is the lineage for the backward-looking CSD / Restitution Bond (B₁) instrument — pricing past extraction that harmed specifically documented people. Separately, stratification economics is the bridge for the heterogeneous/structurally-divided-stakeholder commons that Ostrom cannot reach: the framework applies stratification-economics analysis of hierarchical group-divisions (race, class, ethnicity) to commons simultaneously contested across stakeholder lines AND extracted by structural outsiders, because the CIT operates on the extraction relationship rather than the coordination relationship. Author interviewed Darity (May 2026).
works.where: Heterogeneous-stakeholder break-point (line 604); §15.1.2 CSD lineage (line 6698); §15.2.1 (line 7071); bibliography (Darity interview + JAMA reparations-longevity study)

works.name: Derek Parfit, Reasons and Persons (1984)
works.how_engaged: Supplies the intergenerational moral grounding for the forward-looking Foreclosure Bond (B₂) — moral standing of future people whose specific identities are not knowable in advance (the non-identity problem territory). Paired with option-value economics as the pricing methodology for that standing.

works.name: Dixit & Pindyck, Investment under Uncertainty (1994); Brennan & Schwartz 1985; Arrow & Fisher 1974; Henry 1974
works.how_engaged: Option-value / real-options economics is the methodology lineage for RCV's forward-looking irreversibility pricing (σ + α + V_option parameter set, Method 3 of the Three Ways). The irreversibility-effect literature (Arrow-Fisher, Henry) grounds the asymmetry between reversible non-extraction and irreversible extraction. Single-method RCV is rejected against triangulation.

works.name: Savage 1951 (minimax-regret); Loomes & Sugden 1982 (regret theory); Lempert/Popper/Bankes 2003; Knight 1921; Rio Declaration 1992 Principle 15
works.how_engaged: The Asymmetric Regret Rule (ARR) specializes Savage minimax-regret and Loomes-Sugden regret theory for collective commons-extraction decisions where one direction of regret is commons-extinction. Rejected alternative is standard Cost-Benefit Analysis under risk (Hicks/Kaldor, Mishan, Nordhaus DICE), which fails CBA's three performance conditions for commons (reversibility, expected-value reliability via Knightian deep uncertainty, symmetry). ARR is bidirectional where the precautionary principle (Rio 15) is only unidirectional.

works.name: David Lewis, Counterfactuals (1973)
works.how_engaged: Counterfactual-reasoning methodology is the lineage for the Commons Inversion Test (CIT). The framework specializes one-counterfactual-at-a-time philosophy-of-action reasoning into two sub-forms (CAI + CCI) for commons-extraction discrimination. CIT is chosen over revealed-preference-as-discrimination-gate (Samuelson/Hicks/Bockstael-McConnell) because revealed preference is institutionally captured.

works.name: Costanza et al. 1997/2014 (ecosystem-services valuation); Daily 1997; revealed-preference resource economics (Samuelson 1948, Hicks 1939, Bockstael & McConnell 2007)
works.how_engaged: Replacement-cost ecosystem-services valuation (Costanza) is Method 1 of the Three Ways (anchors a floor, cannot price irreplaceability). Revealed preference (Norway calibration, Method 2) is retained for ESTIMATION but explicitly rejected as a DISCRIMINATION gate because it inherits the pricing polity's institutional architecture. Triangulation discipline grounded in Campbell & Fiske 1959 multitrait-multimethod matrix.

works.name: Mariana Mazzucato, The Value of Everything (2018)
works.how_engaged: Paired with Harvey as the broader "extraction-as-dispossession" / value-capture-vs-value-creation diagnosis that Cost Severance SPECIALIZES for the value-capture-vs-cost-bearing separation in resource-extraction contexts. The HR-severance lexical collision is converted into a teaching on-ramp.

works.name: Sen (Development as Freedom 1999; Inequality Reexamined) & Nussbaum (capabilities approach)
works.how_engaged: The capabilities approach (ten central capabilities held open to refinement) is cited as precedent for the framework's Option C' discipline: presenting the ten commons categories as illustrative examples surfaced from cases rather than an exhaustive metaphysical enumeration.

works.name: Civic-republican tradition: Pettit, Republicanism (1997); Anderson, Private Government (2017); Skinner 1998
works.how_engaged: Cited as one of the divergent political-commitment-spaces the framework's Option C' deliberately accommodates rather than legislating over — civic republicanism treats autonomy as non-domination requiring shared institutions that function as commons. Part of the political-philosophical-accommodation lineage of the configuration claim. Also a §15.2.1 positioning cluster (neo-republican/civic ethics).

works.name: Decolonial / settler-colonial tradition: Coulthard 2014, Tuck & Yang 2012, Wolfe 1999, Whyte; Federici 2004 (Marxist-feminist primitive accumulation); Patel & Moore 2017 (cheap nature); Hickel 2020 (degrowth)
works.how_engaged: Engaged as lineage clusters establishing Cost Severance's cross-political-tradition reach (decolonial extraction, Marxist-feminist reproduction-theory primitive accumulation extending through body/care-work, anti-political-economy cheap-nature). Indigenous-tradition philosophies (relationally-personed ancestral lands) are explicitly named as a commitment-space the framework must accommodate without legislating Western-property commons-ontology. These are positioned as adjacent diagnoses the framework's pricing apparatus serves, not contradicted.

works.name: Nordhaus (DICE 2000) & Stern (Stern Review 2007)
works.how_engaged: The Nordhaus-Stern discount-rate dispute (5.5% vs 1.4%) is engaged directly; the framework does not resolve it but shows via the Weitzman declining-rate result and a sensitivity analysis (McDowell IPG 33-122×) that CS > 0 across the entire plausible range. Nordhaus DICE also named as a single-accounting-object damage-function framework that the two-instrument architecture and ARR are rejected against.

candidate_meta_claims: The framework's distinctive contribution is pricing AND reserving (via foreclosure + restitution bonds) the residual value of a liquidated/foreclosed commons across distance and time — measuring what existing frameworks (Hotelling, Pigou, Coase, Hartwick, Ostrom, Daly, Harvey) each diagnose or partially price but structurally cannot fully capture.

The contribution is configurational, not novelty in any single move: CIT discriminates, Four Gates admit, Three Ways estimate, Two Instruments account, the Hotelling Identity bridges to standard resource economics, Option C' accommodates political-philosophical diversity, and ARR handles irreversibility — each choice's lineage is established and rejected-alternative tested; the integrated configuration applied to commons-extraction is what is new (§15.1.9).

The Hotelling Identity (RCV − Hotelling rent = CS per unit) is the framework's signature formal claim: when extraction honestly prices private exhaustible-resource rent, the residual commons value beyond that rent is precisely the severed cost the extractor is not paying.

The framework prices what cannot be bargained in present markets: future generations cannot bid (extension of Coase), so market pricing systematically under-internalizes the intergenerational component — establishing aggregate B < aggregate RCV as a theorem, hence CS > 0.

The framework reaches commons that the Ostrom tradition structurally cannot: non-renewable permanent-foreclosure commons (no harvest rate to manage) and heterogeneous/structurally-divided-stakeholder commons (via stratification economics), because the Commons Inversion Test operates on the extraction relationship, not the stakeholder-coordination relationship.

Measurement, not new diagnosis, is the value-add against the critical/dispossession traditions (Harvey, Mazzucato, Federici, Patel & Moore): they name the mechanism; RCV/IPG supplies the magnitude — 'it gives the political analysis a number.'

Conservative-by-construction is a deliberate credibility posture: adopting Weitzman declining discount rates biases RCV downward, and CS > 0 is shown robust across the full Nordhaus-to-Stern rate range.

candidate_objections.objection: You are double-counting: the Hotelling rent and the Pigouvian externality together already capture the full social cost, so 'residual commons value beyond Hotelling rent' is just the externality Pigou already prices.
This is just a relabeled Coasean externality / market-incompleteness story dressed in new vocabulary; future-generation under-representation is well known.
Ostrom already showed commons can be governed without pricing; imposing a monetized bond mechanism is exactly the commodifying move the commons literature warns against.
The whole construction is unbounded — any narratable harm becomes an RCV contributor, making the number a vehicle for the analyst's priors.
Discount-rate choice drives the result; pick Nordhaus's 5.5% and the dramatic IPG multiples collapse — the finding is an artifact of the low Weitzman/Stern rate.
Putting a price on dispossession launders an injustice into a transaction; monetizing the commons-loss concedes the extractor's frame and risks legitimating extraction-for-a-fee.
candidate_objections.source: Critical / decolonial / Marxist-feminist traditions (Harvey, Federici, Coulthard, Patel & Moore)

candidate_objections.objection: Revealed preference is the only disciplined valuation input; a counterfactual-driven CIT lets the analyst conjure value no market or polity has ever recognized.
The ten commons categories are arbitrary; without a closed taxonomy the framework can be stretched to price anything.
Cost-benefit analysis already handles uncertainty; minimax-regret/ARR is just precaution-in-disguise that will block any extraction by inflating worst-case regret.

By treating commons-extraction value as something to be priced and capitalized through bonds, you are extending the very financialization/legal-coding logic that turned land and nature into fictitious commodities in the first place.
candidate_objections.source: Polanyi (fictitious commodities), Pistor (Code of Capital), Christophers (asset-economy / rentier capitalism)

notes:
Source file read: /Users/c17n/commons-bonds-pipeline-prose-revision-corpus-audit-260602-6b5eda/manuscript/technical-appendix/TechnicalAppendix_v2.0.0.html (8,044 lines; primary path readable, no fallback needed). The two load-bearing literature-engagement sections are §14 (Engagement with Existing Literature, lines 6522-6622 — seven frameworks each with explicit 'what X gets right / where X stops / formal relationship' structure) and §15.1 (Methodology Defense Consolidation, lines 6648-6963 — eight design choices each with rejected-alternative + cited lineage extension, closing with the §15.1.9 configuration claim). §15.2.1 (lines 6987-7117) adds cross-political-tradition positioning clusters. The Hotelling Identity has its own §4 (lines 1126-1197). Coase/Pigou appear as Theorem premises P1/P2 (lines 3341-3377). Full bibliography is §18 (lines 7716-8044).

IMPORTANT GAP: The three thinkers the task specifically named to search for — Brett CHRISTOPHERS, Katharina PISTOR, and Karl POLANYI — are NOT cited anywhere in the appendix (grep -ci = 0 for all three across the full file, including bibliography). This is a substantive absence, not an extraction miss: I verified by case-insensitive grep over the entire document. These are arguably the most pointed missing interlocutors for a framework whose contribution is pricing+reserving (capitalizing) the residual value of a foreclosed commons — Polanyi's 'fictitious commodities' (land/nature/labor), Pistor's 'Code of Capital' (how legal coding manufactures capital from assets), and Christophers's asset-economy/rentier-capitalism work all directly target the move of turning a commons into a priced/bonded financial object. I flagged this as the single un-engaged candidate objection (engaged: no). The author/editor may want to add an engagement note distinguishing 'pricing the cost severance to force honest accounting and restitution' from 'financializing the commons as a new asset class' — the Polanyi/Pistor/Christophers reading would otherwise be the strongest unanswered hostile-reader line.

The critical/decolonial traditions (Harvey, Mazzucato, Federici, Patel & Moore, Coulthard) ARE cited but engaged mostly as supportive lineage ('they diagnose, we measure'); the framework does not fully engage the internal-to-that-tradition objection that monetization itself concedes the extractor's frame — hence I marked that objection 'partial.'

All relationship classifications use the task's vocabulary; many works carry more than one relationship (e.g., Hotelling is both add-to and extend-into-a-setting-they-could-not-reach) and I noted both in how_engaged.

works.name: Paul Laurence Dunbar ("We Wear the Mask") and W.E.B. Du Bois (the "veil" / double-consciousness)
works.how_engaged: Names the book's central interpretive move as inherited: "The work of naming what hides behind a mask — Dunbar's mask, Du Bois's veil — is older than this book. Black American writing has carried that work for over a century. The framework extends the move into the pricing of commons. The pricing is new; the move is old."

works.name: The free-market / neoclassical economist (voluntary-trade-offs view); "no villain" framing
works.how_engaged: Concedes the free-market reading rather than contradicting it head-on: "A free-market economist would say I made voluntary trade-offs. I did." The system "did exactly what the system was designed to do. There was no villain in it." Sets up the framework as compatible-with but exceeding the voluntary-exchange account. Recurs at Ch5 (consumer not a villain), Ch6 (classical-liberal reading), Ch10.
works.where: Ch1 (lines 90-92); echoed Ch5 Baotou (line 72), Ch6 (autonomy commons readings, lines 243-251)

works.name: Public Choice tradition — James M. Buchanan & Gordon Tullock (rent-seeking; Tullock 1967 "Welfare Costs of Tariffs, Monopolies, and Theft")
works.how_engaged: Treated as complementary-but-asymmetric: Public Choice supplies the vocabulary for WHO shaped the rent-seeking architecture; the framework supplies the ledger of WHAT the architecture cost and to whom. "The two traditions describe the same political-economy phenomenon from two angles, but the angles are not symmetric." Explicitly draws on the rent-seeking vocabulary "rather than endorsing the [Buchanan] project." Names AEI/Cato/Mercatus/GMU as target readers.
works.where: Ch5 "Architecture and rent-seeking" (lines 186-202); Ch9 "Public Choice and the framework" (lines 136-148)

works.name: Ronald Coase ("The Problem of Social Cost," 1960)
works.how_engaged: Engaged as the strongest property-rights objection. Concedes Coasean bargaining works for rivalrous/excludable/present-tense resources, but argues it "structurally cannot reach" three problems: intergenerational foreclosure (future generations "cannot participate in the bargaining Coase described"), transboundary externality, and civilizational-scale information asymmetry. "The residual commons value framework is the supplement, not the replacement."

works.name: Brett Christophers, The Price is Wrong: Why Capitalism Won't Save the Planet (2024)
works.how_engaged: Convergent climate-finance diagnosis: Christophers shows investment is driven by profit not price, so renewables underdeliver absent subsidy. "Christophers names the why; the framework names the how-to-see-it." Both "converge on the same architectural conclusion" re public ownership/investment. First flagged at end of Ch5.

works.name: Adam Smith, The Wealth of Nations
works.how_engaged: Claims lineage rather than rupture: Smith "spent a good portion of The Wealth of Nations arguing against producers who externalized costs onto the public" and "would have recognized what this framework does. It is not a departure from the market mechanism. It is the completion of it."

works.name: Katharina Pistor, The Code of Capital (2019)
works.how_engaged: Capital as legal artifact coded by legal modules. "Where she diagnoses the legal modules that encode cost severance, the framework prices what the modules sever." W.R. Grace's bankruptcy is "a legal-architectural design feature, not a bug." Re-cited in Ch9 as the legal-architectural reason property rights externalize cost.

works.name: Ta-Nehisi Coates, "The Case for Reparations" (The Atlantic, 2014)
works.how_engaged: Coates's argument-architecture (name the mechanism, name the harm, name the debt) is "the rhetorical companion" to reparations economics. "The framework's CSD (Cost Severance Damages) instrument is a structural extension of Coates's argument-architecture into extraction-community contexts."

works.name: William Darity Jr. & A. Kirsten Mullen, From Here to Equality (2020); and reparations economics broadly
works.how_engaged: Named the "contemporary landmark" and "primary informing source." The framework's CSD instrument "applies the same matched-comparison discipline to extraction-community contexts." The Restitution Bond "operationalizes the restitution component of redress within their framework" — "uses their language inside their typology, not against it." Their longevity-gap pricing is the model for McDowell's 13-year gap as a "legacy effect." Backward-direction work "with Darity and Mullen as the primary developers."

works.name: Hamilton, Darity, Price, Sridharan & Tippett, Umbrellas Don't Make It Rain (2015); Dalton Conley, Being Black, Living in the Red (1999); stratification economics
works.how_engaged: Empirical/methodological extension of reparations economics: matched-comparison showing wealth (not behavior) is the structural variable. Conley "established the wealth-as-structural-variable methodology these later works develop." Stratification economics (Darity, Hamilton, collaborators) connected to the framework's heterogeneous-stakeholder commons extension — applying intergroup-extraction analysis to contested commons.

works.name: Derek Parfit, Reasons and Persons (1984) — the non-identity problem; impersonal-outcomes morality
works.how_engaged: Adopted as the philosophical grounding for pricing future-generation costs. "Parfit's response — and the response this framework adopts — is to operate at the level of outcomes rather than of specific persons." RCV "operates in exactly this register." The two-instrument architecture "inherits Parfit's distinction" between forward impersonal and backward person-affecting evaluation. Does not claim to solve the problem Parfit himself did not solve.
works.where: Ch6 "What Is Owed" (lines 257-265, 273); Ch5 (line 224, Parfit 1984 grounding Foreclosure Bond)

works.name: Amartya Sen (and Nussbaum) — capability approach / social-welfare-function
works.how_engaged: Paired with Parfit: "Parfit grounding the standing, Sen grounding the valuation." RCV is "in Sen's vocabulary, a measurement of the capability set extraction forecloses." Sen/Nussbaum's ten capabilities also cited as a precedent for illustrative-not-exhaustive list-making.

works.name: Elinor Ostrom, Governing the Commons (eight design principles)
works.how_engaged: Most sustained boundary-drawing in the book. Distinguishes "coordination commons" (Ostrom's domain) from "extraction commons" (the framework's). "Ostrom operates on coordination commons; the two domains are adjacent and the methodologies extend rather than compete." Names a second break-point Ostrom "does not address": the heterogeneous-stakeholder commons. In "The Contribution," Ostrom (with Pigou) is one of "two intellectual traditions" the framework extends "into a setting neither addressed." Also cited as illustrative-list precedent and in Ch9 property-rights section.
works.where: Ch6 "Two Kinds of Commons" (lines 277-285), "The Contribution" (line 323), RCV-naming (line 325); Ch9 (line 156); Ch9 closing (line 264)

works.name: Arthur Pigou — externality theory
works.how_engaged: Paired with Ostrom as the second tradition the framework extends. "From Pigou comes the move to internalize externalities through pricing instruments." Repeatedly contrasts "standard Pigouvian accounting" (prices damage when it occurs) against RCV (adds substitutability-weighted foreclosure cost + Commons Inversion discovery). "Standard Pigouvian analysis has no principled reason to exclude these costs; it simply has no method for including them. RCV has a method."

works.name: Harold Hotelling (1931 exhaustible-resource economics) and John Hartwick (1977 rule)
works.how_engaged: Hotelling "prices the rent rule for private extraction... but does not price the option-value of leaving resources in situ for future generations." The Hotelling Identity is "the framework's bridge" (RCV minus Hotelling rent = Cost Severance) — "It does not replace either tradition; it formalizes what each leaves out." Hartwick's residual-obligation framing is the lineage for the word "Residual"; Norway is "the closest working example... of Hartwick's Rule."
works.where: Ch6 (lines 331, 325 RCV-naming); Ch5 (line 224 Hartwick 1977); Ch6/Ch9 Norway backtest (Ch6 lines 219-229)

works.name: Mariana Mazzucato — value created vs. value extracted
works.how_engaged: Supplies the "Value" component of "Residual Commons Value": "Mazzucato distinguishes value created from value extracted; the framework's value is what the commons creates over time for the parties who depend on it — a value extraction takes without paying for."

works.name: Martin Weitzman (declining discount rate; 2009 fat-tail climate damages)
works.how_engaged: Discount factor D(t,t0) "follow[s] Weitzman's declining-rate framework." The "externality tail" usage joins "the statistical-distribution lineage that climate-tipping-point and climate-tail-risk literatures occupy; Weitzman's 2009 work on fat-tail climate damages is the adjacent tradition the usage joins." Also named (Ch10) as proposing a declining-rate direction "without ending the argument."
works.where: Ch6 RCV formula (line 119), externality-tail naming (line 121); Ch10 (line 76)

works.name: Stern Review (Nicholas Stern) vs. William Nordhaus (DICE) — discount-rate debate; Ramsey (1928)
works.how_engaged: Cited as the unresolved century-long discount-rate impasse the substitutability function "sidesteps": "Instead of asking 'how much do future generations matter?' it asks 'how quickly can we develop alternatives?'" The model "is honest about this. It does not pretend to settle a question the discipline has not settled."

works.name: Dixit & Pindyck, Investment Under Uncertainty (1994) — real options
works.how_engaged: Method 3 (Scarcity-Adjusted Option Value) applies "Dixit and Pindyck's 1994 real-options framework with the framework's scarcity multiplier... and irreversibility premium." Standard real-options is the mainstream model oil majors already use; the framework "add[s] the social terms."

works.name: Leonard Savage (1951) — minimax-regret
works.how_engaged: The Asymmetric Regret Rule "specializes Savage 1951's minimax-regret discipline to commons-extraction decisions where one direction of regret is commons-extinction." Does not replace CBA where CBA works; specializes for the case-class where CBA's symmetry assumption fails.
works.where: Ch6 (lines 359-361); applied in Ch9 Step 2 reservation (line 56) and CCZ case (line 236)

works.name: Philip Pettit, Republicanism (1997); Quentin Skinner, Liberty Before Liberalism (1998); Elizabeth Anderson — civic-republican tradition (freedom as non-domination)
works.how_engaged: Grounds the Foreclosure Bond's commitment to future generations: a society extracting in ways that subject future generations to domination "violates the civic-republican commitment." Framework "does not require the reader to accept civic-republican political philosophy as the only frame" — engages it "under the discipline of accommodating where alternative traditions converge." Civic-republican autonomy-as-non-domination is one of the multiple readings the autonomy-commons accommodates.

works.name: Mancur Olson, The Logic of Collective Action (1965)
works.how_engaged: Diagnoses why honest pricing has not happened: concentrated interests defeat diffuse interests. "This is not a discovery. Mancur Olson described it in 1965." The framework's contribution is changing the terms (moral argument to fiscal; concrete local beneficiaries).

works.name: Atif Mian & Amir Sufi, House of Debt (2014)
works.how_engaged: Their welfare calculation of the 2008 household-debt-restructuring counterfactual is cited as quantifying the cost of the chosen bank-rescue architecture; "the framework's accountability-bond logic prices [the scope-choice] structurally."

works.name: Jacob Hacker, The Great Risk Shift (2006); Mishel & Bivens (EPI, productivity-wage decoupling)
works.how_engaged: Hacker "named the broader pattern of risk-and-cost transfer from institutional to individual balance sheets that healthcare-cost-severance is one specific instance of." Mishel/Bivens document the wage-productivity gap that makes households unable to absorb the transfer.

works.name: Atul Gawande, Being Mortal (2014); Katy Butler (journalist, pacemaker case)
works.how_engaged: Gawande "documents the broader structure" of systematic over-treatment of the dying / under-funded palliative care; Butler's documented pacemaker case is "one archetype of a systemic pattern" of cost severance "operating on the dying body."

works.name: Kindleberger (1973) & Eichengreen (1992) — comparative banking-crisis literature; the 1930s Swedish response
works.how_engaged: Cited as establishing that "bank-rescue and household-relief are not structurally either-or." The Swedish 1930s response is the positive analog to the contingent US 2008 scope-choice; "what was chosen once can be re-decided."

works.name: David Lewis — counterfactual reasoning
works.how_engaged: The Commons Inversion Test "is the David Lewis counterfactual-reasoning tradition specialized to one specific premise-flip" — inverting only the resource-scarcity dimension while holding the world fixed.

works.name: David Harvey — "accumulation by dispossession"
works.how_engaged: Named as a precedent for what frameworks (vs. legislation) do: "did not pass a single law. It gave people a vocabulary for naming a structural pattern" — model for how Commons Bonds expects to operate.

works.name: Kate Raworth, Doughnut Economics; Matthew Desmond, Evicted; Daniel Susskind, Growth: A Reckoning (2024)
works.how_engaged: Cited as model cases of frameworks/narratives that changed the conceivable without passing laws. Susskind specifically aligned: "Susskind's growth-redirection [and] the framework's accounting... are different facets of the same task — making honest what current measurement leaves dishonest."

works.name: Garrett Hardin — "The Tragedy of the Commons"
works.how_engaged: Invoked through Ostrom: the coordination-commons risk "Ostrom names is that uncoordinated drawing produces collective ruin — Hardin's 'tragedy' — and her contribution was to demonstrate, empirically across hundreds of cases, that communities... can self-govern." The book sides with Ostrom's empirical refutation.

works.name: Indigenous-studies / decolonial tradition — Robin Wall Kimmerer, Leanne Betasamosake Simpson, Glen Coulthard, Roxanne Dunbar-Ortiz, Vine Deloria Jr.
works.how_engaged: Explicitly named as the tradition the framework CANNOT reach. The Black Hills / Lakota case (US v. Sioux Nation 1980) is "in the category the framework cannot price." "A reader who wants to understand what the framework cannot reach should read [them]... It is a different shape of the question the book is asking, and a deeper one." Frames itself as settler-authored; structural-mechanism claim, not moral equivalence.

works.name: Albert Camus, Le Mythe de Sisyphe (1942)
works.how_engaged: Closing metaphor. "The framework... is not the boulder... It does not lighten the load... What it does is tell him where north is." Frames the framework as a compass, not a redemption.

works.name: Marxist / radical-left anti-capitalist critique (the "does not go far enough" objection)
works.how_engaged: Engaged as "the objection I find most serious," from readers who are "in a real sense, my allies." Concedes the framework "can be captured... turned into an ESG slogan," but insists cost severance "is not a feature of any ownership structure" — feudal lords, pharaohs, Qin emperors did it; "Capitalism is the current and most efficient delivery vehicle. It is not the cause." Refuses to take the pro- or anti-capitalist side.

candidate_meta_claims: The book's master move is OLD (naming what hides behind a mask — Dunbar's veil, Du Bois's mask, a century of Black American writing) applied to a NEW object (the pricing of commons): "The pricing is new; the move is old."

The framework EXTENDS rather than rejects the two parent traditions it grew from — Pigouvian externality theory and Ostrom-lineage commons governance — "into a setting neither addressed: extraction contexts where commons are drawn down rather than managed-in-place."

Three contributions claimed as net-new relative to that lineage: (1) substitutability-weighting of foreclosure cost (a category standard externality analysis lacks); (2) an integrated additive formula (RCV) uniting foreclosure + externality + community + epistemic terms under shared gates; (3) the Commons Inversion Test as a generative discovery method for previously-unpriced costs.

Methodological credibility rests on TRIANGULATION: three independent estimation methods (Replacement Cost / Revealed Preference / Scarcity-Adjusted Option Value) built on different foundations converge in direction (CS>0) across every tested case — convergence as evidence the measured thing is real, not a parameter artifact.

The framework is DIRECTION-AGNOSTIC: the same apparatus (four gates, three methods) runs forward (Foreclosure Bond, impersonal-outcomes, environmental-bond economics) and backward (Restitution Bond, person-affecting harm, reparations economics) — inheriting Parfit's forward/backward distinction.

The framework is a complement, not a competitor, to Public Choice: Public Choice analyzes WHO shaped rent-seeking architectures; the framework supplies the LEDGER of what those architectures cost and to whom. It draws on rent-seeking vocabulary without endorsing Buchanan's broader political project.

The framework is politically non-aligned by design: it measures what extraction costs; cost severance is a structural mechanism independent of ownership structure (feudal, capitalist, state-owned, cooperative), so it takes neither the pro- nor anti-capitalist side.

The framework structures uncertainty rather than eliminating it — converting an unresolvable philosophical question (the discount rate) into a tractable empirical one (substitutability), and disclosing parameter sensitivity rather than hiding behind a single headline number.

Honest pricing is reachable, not utopian: every component (carbon pricing, sovereign wealth funds, reclamation bonds, critical-minerals stockpiles, EIAs, border carbon adjustment, Alaska/Norway funds, Chattanooga/Mondragon/Vienna) already exists as a partial "entering wedge"; the task is to connect, strengthen, align, and extend them.

The framework names its own limits as a discipline: it cannot settle the discount rate, cannot price the non-denominable (a Europa biosphere, a sacred landscape), and explicitly cannot reach indigenous dispossession — the Black Hills is "the account that holds the framework honest."

candidate_objections.objection: The model's output depends on analyst parameter choices — "choose your own adventure"; different choices yield different numbers.
A model that always finds extraction underpriced is unfalsifiable (Popperian objection).
Two analysts applying the framework to the same case won't produce the same number (reproducibility).
The substitutability function does enormous work and merely relocates the hard problem from the discount rate to a substitutability curve.
candidate_objections.source: Critic of the RCV model's central move

candidate_objections.objection: How can extraction wrong people who do not yet exist? (the non-identity problem)
Well-defined private property rights plus Coasean bargaining already solve the commons problem.
Borrowers took loans they could not afford — they had agency (2008).
2008 was a global crisis, not a US-policy failure.
TARP was repaid, so taxpayers did not lose money.
Dodd-Frank already reformed the architecture (the reformist objection).
The Social Security Treasury-bond mechanism was administratively prudent, not the source of severance.
The Social Security gap is a tractable, soluble policy problem (timeline-and-tractability).
candidate_objections.source: Bipartisan Policy Center / Simpson-Bowles / Concord Coalition fiscal-policy reader

candidate_objections.objection: Nordic outcomes are not replicable in a continental, demographically heterogeneous democracy of 330M (scale, homogeneity, resource base, historical contingency).
If a simple carbon tax can't pass after 30 years, a comprehensive RCV regime is fantasy — show me the legislative pathway, the coalition, the treaty.
candidate_objections.source: Practitioner who has tried to pass legislation

candidate_objections.objection: The book does not go far enough — it is a reformist critique a capitalism that extracts-until-nothing-is-left will swallow and accommodate.
The book is too generous to institutional reform / too statist.
Treating indigenous dispossession as one case among many (coal mines, oil spills) flattens what is being engaged.
Is the Commons Inversion Test filtering the same commons Ostrom's Governing the Commons describes — i.e., does the framework compete with Ostrom?
The framework can be captured — turned into an ESG slogan / conflict-free-diamond performance of accountability.

Read from the audit-corpus dir (exists): /Users/c17n/commons-bonds-pipeline-prose-revision-corpus-audit-260602-6b5eda/manuscript/chapters/ — files Chapter__1_TheQuietMath.md, Chapter__5_TheAccountabilityGap.md, Chapter__6_ThreeWaysofCounting.md, Chapter__9_PricingHonestly.md, Chapter_10_CommonBonds.md.

Density of positioning by chapter: Ch1 is thin — a single load-bearing lineage claim (Dunbar/Du Bois mask) plus the free-market/no-villain framing. Ch5 and Ch6 are where the book situates itself in the academic literature most densely (reparations economics, Pistor, Parfit, Sen, civic-republican, Public Choice, Ostrom, Pigou, Hotelling/Hartwick, Mazzucato). Ch9 carries the policy-economy positioning (Coase/property-rights, Christophers, Olson, Mian & Sufi, Smith, Nordic objection, framework-vs-legislation precedents). Ch10 carries the meta-positioning + the explicit out-of-scope boundary (Black Hills / indigenous tradition) + the anti-capitalist "doesn't go far enough" objection + the Camus compass metaphor.

Recurring rhetorical pattern worth flagging for the contribution-claim audit: nearly every key TERM (Restitution Bond, Foreclosure Bond, Residual Commons Value, externality tail, Commons Inversion Test, Asymmetric Regret Rule, bond-as-umbrella) gets an explicit "other names were available and each fails for reason X; this name joins tradition Y" passage. These naming-justification passages are the single richest source of "the book claims continuity with tradition Z" signals and are concentrated in Ch5 (bond-naming, lines 212-228) and Ch6 (RCV/CIT/ARR naming, lines 121, 303, 325, 361).

The relationship taxonomy in this audit: I used "extend-into-a-setting-they-could-not-reach" wherever the book explicitly says it extends a tradition into territory that tradition did not/could not address (Pigou, Ostrom, Coase, Hotelling/Hartwick, Pistor, Dunbar/Du Bois, Coates, Dixit-Pindyck, Savage, Lewis). "add-to" for sources whose findings are cited as convergent support without an explicit reach-claim (Darity/Mullen, Hacker, Gawande, Olson, Mian/Sufi, Christophers, Susskind, Mazzucato, Weitzman). "parallel" for traditions engaged as compatible-but-distinct frames the framework accommodates rather than absorbs (Public Choice, civic-republican, free-market economist, Parfit, Harvey/Raworth/Desmond as framework-precedents, Marxist objection). "contradict" only for Hardin (the book sides with Ostrom's empirical refutation). "unclear" for the indigenous tradition, which the book deliberately places outside its reach rather than in any add/extend relationship.

Objection-engagement note: the book is unusually systematic about steelmanning — Ch5 and the SS/2008 sections run formal "serious version of the objection / framework's response" structures, and Ch9's Nordic objection runs four named sub-conditions each separately rebutted. I marked the non-identity, indigenous-flattening, and too-statist objections "partial" because the book explicitly declines to fully resolve them (Parfit's problem it concedes is unsolved; indigenous dispossession it concedes is out of scope; the conservative objection is named in one line at Ch10:126 but not separately rebutted the way the left objection is).

works.name: Katharina Pistor, The Code of Capital

works.name: Mariana Mazzucato, The Value of Everything / value creation vs. value extraction
works.how_engaged: Explicit crisp formula: 'Mazzucato names the extraction. The framework I am proposing adds the accounting.' Mazzucato draws the making/taking distinction but provides a diagnostic frame, not a metric; the framework supplies the per-transaction accounting/instrumentation that operationalizes it.
works.where: Noema 'Commons Bonds' §VI (verbatim 'Mazzucato names the extraction. The framework I am proposing adds the accounting'); NYRB (second book reviewed); Harper's §on vocabulary lineage

works.name: Brett Christophers, The Price Is Wrong
works.how_engaged: Christophers diagnoses that 'profit, not price' governs capital allocation and that prices fail to align private return with public goods; the framework treats this as the mechanism-level evidence the corrective instrument would attach to ('prices the architecture produces are not natural facts but products of how the code is written, a point Christophers develops').
works.where: NYRB (third book reviewed); Public Books §'one corner of the system that almost works' close

works.name: Daniel Susskind, Growth: A Reckoning

works.name: John Hartwick / Hartwick's rule (1977 AER)
works.how_engaged: Hartwick proved rents can be reinvested to sustain consumption, but '[t]he rule operates on the consumption stream. It does not reconstitute the stock.' The framework's 'residual commons value' / 'foreclosure component' prices precisely what Hartwick conserves the proceeds of but never priced: the permanently foreclosed barrel itself. '$100 Barrel' makes this the climactic near-miss.

works.name: Elinor Ostrom, Governing the Commons
works.how_engaged: Ostrom governed RENEWABLE commons (fisheries, forests, aquifers) and her design principles presuppose a sustainable harvest; oil 'sits outside the world her principles were built for' because the non-renewable resource cannot be sustained at any harvest. NYRB additionally uses Ostrom's homogeneity-of-interest condition as a constraint Pistor's account leaves open.
works.where: $100 Barrel §'A lineage of near-misses' (Ostrom 'governed the renewable'); Atlantic Ideas 'four traditions' §; NYRB §on Pistor's scope limit + lineage para

works.name: Herman Daly / steady-state + ecological economics

works.name: Karl Marx (Capital, surplus value)
works.how_engaged: Marx's injustice runs 'along the axis of class'; the framework's runs 'along the axis of time.' The barrel could be burned with surplus split perfectly evenly and still be gone forever — Marx names a real but different injustice. Atlantic Ideas pointedly substitutes Polanyi for Marx in the 'four traditions' gloss.
works.where: $100 Barrel §'A lineage of near-misses' (opening with Marx 'as a tell'); Atlantic Ideas provenance note ('Polanyi-not-Marx four-traditions gloss')

works.name: John Rawls (A Theory of Justice / veil of ignorance)
works.how_engaged: Borrows the veil-of-ignorance device and 'widens the veil' along the generational dimension — you don't know which generation you're born into — to derive the claim that residual commons value must be reckoned. works.how_engaged: Convergent corroboration: Sandel's distinction between what markets can price and what they cannot is one of 'three thinkers, three starting points, one convergent result' supporting that the missing number is real and not an artifact of one philosopher's commitments.

works.name: Derek Parfit (Reasons and Persons / population ethics)
works.how_engaged: Supplies the logic of obligations to people who do not yet exist; used as both convergent corroboration ($100 Barrel) and the philosophical grounding for the forward-looking Foreclosure Bond (Public Books).

candidate_meta_claims: The central repeated contribution claim, in its crispest verbatim form (Noema §VI): 'Mazzucato names the extraction. The framework I am proposing adds the accounting.' The framework's distinctive move is supplying a PER-TRANSACTION pricing instrument/accounting, not a new diagnosis.

From the NYRB synthesis (the sharpest articulation the chapters likely lack): adjacent thinkers have named 'the location [Pistor], the activity [Mazzucato], the mechanism [Christophers], and the metric [Susskind]' of an engineered architecture of cost severance, but none 'operationalize the corrective at the per-extraction-event mechanism level.' The framework is 'the assembly' — building and seating the instruments the four diagnoses make legible. 'The shape of what is missing... is no longer a matter of speculation. It is a matter of construction.'

Cost severance is the load-bearing named mechanism: 'the structural separation of the party that captures the value of an activity from the party that absorbs its costs, where the second party has less power than the first to refuse the transfer.' Crucially distinguished from 'externality': 'An externality names a cost that falls outside a transaction; cost severance names the legal architecture that put it there, and the power asymmetry that keeps the cost-bearer from refusing it' (Public Books). The framework's edge over standard economics is naming the architecture + the power asymmetry, not just the unpriced cost.

The 'lineage of near-misses' contribution frame ($100 Barrel): a century of major economists each 'worked the ground around the empty plot' — Marx priced the surplus, Ostrom governed the renewable, Daly warned about throughput, Hartwick saved the rent, Stern/Nordhaus/Weitzman argued the discount rate — but none priced 'the residual commons value,' the worth of what is permanently foreclosed for the unborn. This is the cleanest articulation of WHAT IS NEW: pricing the permanent absence of a non-renewable resource, a quantity that lives outside the discount-rate axis and survives even a low discount rate because it is not a discounted future flow.

The bidirectional-accountability articulation (Public Books): cost severance resolves into two instruments pointing opposite directions in time — the backward-looking Restitution Bond (prices harm already realized; operationalizes the material-restitution component of reparations economics and extends it) and the forward-looking Foreclosure Bond (bars present extraction from foreclosing the unborn's options; grounded in Hartwick + Parfit + civic-republican non-domination). Norway = the Foreclosure Bond 'in working form.'

The architecture-is-a-choice meta-claim, repeated as the organizing existence-proof logic: cost severance 'is a policy choice. It is not a geological fact' (Noema/Foreign Affairs). Norway vs. Nigeria/Venezuela = 'same crude, different architecture, different outcome.' Darity/Mullen = the cost-side existence proof from the opposite end of a century. 'What is engineered can be engineered differently.'

The honest-pricing-as-completion-of-the-market framing (Atlantic Ideas): the framework is 'not a departure from the market... It is the completion of it. A price that reflects every cost is the price the market was always supposed to produce.' Four operational moves: Classify (substitution gap), Reserve, Invest, Border. This is the most policy-instrument-forward articulation.

The non-partisanship/structural-not-prescriptive meta-claim, present in every essay's close: the argument 'is structural, not partisan'; the framework 'offers the ledger,' not a legislative program; reachable from regulatory caution, market discipline, OR worker solidarity. 'A free-market system that does not price its costs is not a free-market system. It is a transfer mechanism' (Harper's).

The framework requires 'no new philosophical foundations — the intellectual foundations have been built' (NYRB): reparations economics for the backward instrument, civic-republican non-domination for the forward instrument, Hartwick + Norway for the operational template, Pistor's modules as the coding location. The novelty is assembly + per-transaction instrumentation, not invention of new theory.

candidate_objections.objection: The framework is just a rebranding of standard externality theory / Pigouvian taxation with new vocabulary.
If you cannot produce the actual number, you have only named a gap, not closed it — naming is not pricing.
This is the substitution argument: scarcity raises prices, which summons alternatives, so there is no missing price; the future is handled by the same mechanism that handles the present.
You are simply taking a side in the Stern-Nordhaus discount-rate debate dressed up as a new quantity.
Norway is a small, homogeneous, high-trust, low-corruption society with a freak windfall; the model is a Norwegian particular, not a transferable existence proof.
The Restitution Bond appropriates or dilutes the specific historical claim of reparations by folding it into a generic cost-severance class (asbestos, oil).
'He chose those hours / the miner chose the mine' — choice is real, so cost severance overstates structural coercion.
This is a left-wing anti-extraction / anti-market program disguised as accounting; a call for revolution.
Honest pricing is rich-world protectionism — pulling the cheap-energy ladder up behind developing economies.
The arrangement persists because concentrated interests capture the rules (rent-seeking); naming a ledger does nothing against that political economy.
A border-adjustment / extended-cost-pricing mechanism is administratively unbuildable beyond carbon (embedded carbon is measurable; most severed costs are not).

The Norway sovereign-fund model is not generalizable: if every resource state ran one investing abroad, there would be insufficient external activity to absorb the aggregate — it is a local optimum dependent on others not copying it.

WHERE THE ESSAYS ARTICULATE THE CONTRIBUTION MORE CRISPLY THAN THE CHAPTERS LIKELY DO:
1. NYRB 'The Architecture and Its Residue' is the single richest positioning document: it does the explicit four-thinker triangulation (Pistor=location, Mazzucato=activity, Christophers=mechanism, Susskind=metric) and states the framework's job as 'the assembly' of an instrument the four diagnoses make legible but none builds. The 'negative-space observations' (none offers a per-transaction pricing instrument; none connects present extraction to future cost-bearer at the transaction level) are the cleanest statement of the contribution's boundary anywhere in the corpus. It also names the mid-century-pivot analogy (General Theory / Great Transformation / Road to Serfdom).
2. $100 Barrel 'lineage of near-misses' is the cleanest articulation of WHAT IS NEW analytically: residual commons value = the price of the permanently foreclosed non-renewable resource, a quantity that lives OUTSIDE the discount-rate axis (the move that distinguishes it from a Stern-Nordhaus vote). The renewables-return-near-zero symmetry is offered as proof it is 'an accounting and not an argument in disguise.'
3. Noema's 'Mazzucato names the extraction. The framework I am proposing adds the accounting.' is the most compressed single-sentence contribution claim.
4. Public Books gives the cleanest mechanism definition + the externality-vs-cost-severance distinction (adds 'the legal architecture that put it there, and the power asymmetry') and the bidirectional Restitution Bond / Foreclosure Bond instrument split with named lineages for each.
5. Atlantic Ideas gives the most policy-instrument-forward articulation (Classify/Reserve/Invest/Border) and the 'honest pricing = completion of the market Smith described' framing.

NAMED INSTRUMENTS appear in different guises by venue: 'cost severance' is the universal named mechanism (plain prose in all). 'Residual commons value / RCV' surfaces in $100 Barrel + Foreign Affairs. 'Accountability bond B' + 'foreclosure component' + 'externality tail' are Foreign-Affairs-specific framework vocabulary. 'Restitution Bond' + 'Foreclosure Bond' are the Public Books named instruments. Op-eds and the two Atlantic essays deliberately carry ZERO named framework apparatus (per brief exclusion lists) and convey the concept via 'honest pricing / institutional architecture is engineerable.'

RECURRING DISARMING MOVES worth flagging: (a) explicit refusal to take Stern's or Nordhaus's side; (b) explicit Rawls-tool-not-Rawls-program hedge; (c) Polanyi substituted for Marx in Atlantic Ideas; (d) first-person deferral on reparations ('not mine to do'); (e) 'structural not partisan' + 'not a call for revolution' in every close; (f) Public Choice/rent-seeking folded in as 'complementary, in the toolkit, not the opposing camp.' These suggest the author treats the center-right/non-partisan reader and the reparations scholar as the two most load-bearing adversarial audiences."

works.name: Karl Polanyi — The Great Transformation (1944): fictitious commodities (labor, land, money)
works.how_engaged: The framework's STRUCTURAL definition of its object is the operational descendant of Polanyi's fictitious-commodity thesis: labor/land/money are treated as commodities though not produced as such. Commons Bonds reframes its 10 elements (Habitability, Ecosystem, Temporal, Autonomy, Kindred, etc.) as COMMONS — things depended-on and shared but not produced-as-commodities — and the Commons Inversion Test operationalizes Polanyi's intuition by asking 'imagine this commons absent/unconsumed; what cost appears?' Crucially Polanyi is named only as background lineage and as a 'Pattern 3 ex-post-only' diagnostic precedent — engagement is thin. Polanyi diagnosed the double movement and the disembedding of fictitious commodities but priced nothing; the framework EXTENDS Polanyi into the pricing/accountability-instrument setting Polanyi never reached (RCV quantification + Accountability Bond B = B1 restitution + B2 foreclosure). Note: Polanyi is NOT a formal bibliography entry in Tech Appendix v2.0.0 and 'fictitious commodities' is engaged only obliquely; this is an under-realized positioning opportunity.
works.where: commons-as-structural-identity rigor pass §7 (ratified 2026-04-24, lineage citation Ostrom+Hess+Klinenberg+Anderson+Polanyi 1944); commons-list-dissolution pass §4.2 (Polanyi's 3 fictitious commodities cited as enumerated-framework precedent); alignment/methodology/decision_time_application_internal_v1.0.0.md §3.3 (Polanyi as Pattern-3 ex-post-only exemplar); full-framework-audit-phase1 §M12 (Polanyi-tradition lineage)

works.name: Nancy Fraser — expropriation of social reproduction / nature (Cannibal Capitalism; 'Behind Marx's Hidden Abode')

Zero hits for Fraser the political theorist, 'social reproduction,' or Fraser-style 'expropriation' anywhere in core/, chapters/, or tools/ (all 'Fraser' string-matches are Simon Fraser University and the Peters Fraser + Dunlop literary agency; all 'expropriation' hits are the LIBERTARIAN HOSTILE READ of the bonds-architecture as property-rights-expropriation, the opposite of Fraser's technical sense). This is the single biggest positioning gap. Fraser's expropriation/exploitation distinction — capital's reliance on the unpaid appropriation of social-reproductive labor and nature 'behind' the wage relation — is almost exactly the framework's claim that cost severance prices what markets push onto the unpaid commons substrate (community, future generations, ecosystem). The framework already does the parallel work empirically (Federici, Patel & Moore are cited for the same substrate) but never names Fraser, who is the closest living political-theory anchor for the meta-claim. Adding Fraser would let the book position its object-definition as the pricing-instrument extension of Fraser's diagnostic.

works.name: Elinor Ostrom — Governing the Commons (1990); Hess & Ostrom — Understanding Knowledge as a Commons (2007)
works.how_engaged: Primary lineage anchor. The framework's structural identity IS 'commons-governance extended to extraction contexts.' Ostrom governs commons that PERSIST (8 design principles for in-place management among aligned stakeholders); the framework prices commons that are DRAWN DOWN via extraction and explicitly reaches the heterogeneous/divided-stakeholder commons Ostrom's apparatus excludes (TA §1.10 break-point → stratification economics). The Commons Inversion Test is positioned as a framework-specific methodology with no Ostrom-literature prior art.

candidate_meta_claims: The framework's OBJECT is defined STRUCTURALLY, not by enumeration: a commons is anything depended-on and shared but not produced-as-a-commodity (Polanyi's fictitious-commodity logic generalized). Therefore a community, a people, a body of labor, and a future generation are commons in exactly the same sense as a reef or the atmosphere.

Because the object is structural, ecological extraction (strip-mining a reef / atmosphere) and human extraction (strip-mining a community; slavery/forced labor; the intergenerational wealth gap) are the SAME OBJECT — a commons whose abundance masked its cost — and the same apparatus (Four Gates + Three Ways of Counting) prices both, running FORWARD for ecological/atmospheric cases and BACKWARD (in reverse) for realized human-extraction / reparations cases. (Tech Appendix §5.5, line 1385: 'The same apparatus applies in reverse to price backward damages.')

The Commons Inversion Test makes the structural identity operational: invert the abundance of any commons (imagine it absent or unconsumed) and the masked, severed cost becomes legible — oxygen in space, water on Mars, time consumed by commuting, freedom stripped by coercion all yield to one test.

The book's title resolves on the shared bond of the extracted-from: 'Commons Bonds' is load-bearing polysemy — (1) the accountability BONDS (instrument B) that would price the severance, (2) the relational BONDS (intergenerational, community, kinship, labor solidarity) that severance breaks, and (3) the structural BONDS of ecosystems/economies. The three are mechanistically linked: posting the accountability bond is what closes the gap that severs the relational and structural bonds. The unifying subject is whoever/whatever was extracted from — reef, community, generation — bound together as commons.

The framework is positioned as commons-governance (Ostrom) EXTENDED into extraction-drawdown contexts that Ostrom governs-in-place could not reach, fused with a pricing instrument (RCV) and an accountability bond (B1 restitution + B2 foreclosure) that the Polanyi/Marx/Harvey/Mazzucato diagnostic traditions never supplied — the contribution is the pricing/accountability layer on top of a structural-extraction diagnosis those traditions share.

Universality is a claim about METHOD, not ontology: the framework does not assert what metaphysically IS a commons; it asserts that whenever something operates AS a commons in a given society, extraction can sever cost from it, and this methodology measures the severance — working within classical-liberal, civic-republican, socialist-communitarian, Marxist, or decolonial commitments alike. What it refuses is extraction without accountability, regardless of which commons.

candidate_objections.objection: You claim Polanyi lineage but engage him only as background — you have not actually built on the fictitious-commodity thesis, you have re-derived a thinner version of it and dropped his name in a footnote. Where is the genuine extension vs. the rebrand?

The single closest living anchor for your central meta-claim — that capital relies on the unpaid appropriation of social-reproductive labor and nature 'behind' the wage relation — is Nancy Fraser's expropriation/exploitation distinction, and she is nowhere in the book. Treating reef-extraction and community-extraction as 'the same object priced backward' is precisely Fraser's terrain; not engaging her reads as either ignorance of the field or avoidance.

Collapsing ecological extraction and human extraction into 'the same object' flattens the coercion vector. Slavery is not a reef with a longer time-horizon; the moral structure of coerced human extraction is categorically different from drawing down a fishery, and pricing it 'the same way, backward' risks commodifying exactly the unpriceable.
candidate_objections.source: Reparations economics (Darity) + decolonial tradition (Coulthard, Tuck & Yang)

candidate_objections.objection: Treating 'a community' or 'a people' as a commons identical in structure to the atmosphere is an ontological overreach — it imports a collectivist premise about what is shared that classical-liberal and property-rights traditions reject categorically; the 'structural identity' move smuggles a political commitment in under the guise of accounting.

'Cost severance' borrows HR/employment-severance and severance-tax connotations that read as commodifying-labor framing rather than commons-extraction framing — for indigenous and heterodox-left readers the metaphor itself enacts the disembedding it claims to critique.

This is just externality internalization / Pigouvian pricing with a commons vocabulary layered on; the 'structural identity' and 'same object' claims add rhetoric, not analytical content the welfare-economics tradition lacks.

POSITIONING vs FRASER: the biggest gap. Nancy Fraser is entirely absent from the corpus (verified by repo-wide grep; every 'Fraser'/'expropriation' hit is a false positive — Simon Fraser Univ, PFD agency, or the LIBERTARIAN hostile-read of bonds-as-property-expropriation, which is the inverse of Fraser's sense). Fraser's expropriation-of-social-reproduction/nature is the single closest living political-theory anchor for the same-object meta-claim and would let the book name its parallel directly. The empirical substrate Fraser theorizes is already cited (Federici 2004, Patel & Moore 2017) but the synthesizing theorist is not. STRONG candidate for a Stage-1b bibliography + Ch-1/Ch-6/TA engagement addition.

Relationship to political-economy traditions overall: ADD-TO the diagnostic traditions (Polanyi, Marx, Harvey, Mazzucato, Fraser-by-omission all diagnose structural extraction); the framework's specific contribution is the PRICING + ACCOUNTABILITY-INSTRUMENT layer (RCV + B = B1 + B2 + bidirectional Three Ways of Counting) that none of those traditions supply.
